Then he descends — a diving kick wreathed in the deployed caste, the stick still between his fingers, the grin visible from the ground. The swarm arrives a heartbeat before his heel. The heel arrives a heartbeat before consciousness leaves. Candy-red energy streams around his descending leg and bursts on impact. He catches the lollipop on the way down.

Mirage Art
Mango Tango
▾
Tango de Mango
MirageCombat

Mirage class insects flood the air with holographic projections — false openings, phantom Ajays stepping left when he steps right, and projected signs that flash "LOSER" and "WRONG WAY" in bright neon as the opponent swings at nothing. The rage-bait is deliberate. Every taunt makes them overcommit, swing wild, abandon their guard. Ajay dances with it. Every wild swing at a phantom gets a smooth deflection and a counter from the real one, redirecting them into their own momentum. He's leading. They're following. The rhythm accelerates — opponent wilder, Ajay smoother, the contrast absurd. Then the projections cut. The music stops. Spinning back kick to the ribs. Deep mango-orange energy streams around him throughout and bursts warm at the final kick. One partner leaves the floor.

Elemental Swarm
Jellyfish Jam
▾
Mermelada de Medusa
Elemental

Elemental jellyfish ascend and disperse — a drifting canopy of bioluminescent light. Each one is a charged weapon: electrical discharge, thermal burst, cryo-pulse. Ajay fights beneath the canopy using Geppo to launch combinations that drive opponents upward into it. When he commands, the canopy collapses and the aurora becomes a storm. Electric blue-purple energy streams around his legs and bursts bright at each impact that sends someone skyward.

Sovereign Command
Sugar Rush
▾
Corrida de Azúcar
CombatRokushikiSoldierChrysalisHerculeanSteel Council

Everything at once. Every system fires. The swarm deploys at maximum doctrine — every caste, every specialist line. Soldier formations lock the perimeter. Chrysalis units iterate in real-time. Herculean shields crash through guard stances. Steel Council anchors the kill zone. Simultaneously, Ajay launches into the fastest combination Sunburst Tropicana has — Cherry Rush Burst speed, Honeycomb Blitz coverage, Banana Split disorientation, compressed into a single sustained assault. He's everywhere. The swarm is everywhere. Every colour cycles through in rapid streams — orange, cherry, pink, yellow, violet, amber — bursting at every point of contact. Not one technique. All of them. Maximum volume. It ends when everything is quiet.

Compound Combat Art
Pear Daiquiri
▾
Daiquiri de Pera
CombatRokushikiChrysalisWorkerPollen

It starts with a kick. One kick — a rising crescent from the ground that catches the target under the chin and sends them skyward. The kind of kick that Rock Lee would respect. Ajay doesn't watch them fly — he's already moving. Geppo launches him after them, and what follows is the fastest sustained aerial combination Sunburst Tropicana has ever produced. He Geppos around the target at blistering speed, kicking from every conceivable angle — left, right, above, below, behind — each strike a different technique, each one landing before the target can process the last. Chrysalis units iterate his attack pattern in real-time, feeding the Archivist counter-adaptation data that adjusts each subsequent kick to hit harder than the one before. Worker bees spiral around the assault in a golden helix, documenting and amplifying. Pollen-class insects cloud the air in a luminous yellow haze that tracks the target's every twitch and feeds positional data back to Ajay mid-Geppo. The tempo is inhuman. The rhythm is relentless. Pear-green energy streams around his legs and bursts bright at every impact. Then the finale — Ajay appears directly above, silhouetted against the pollen-lit sky, and drops. A descending axe kick with the full weight of gravity, momentum, and every caste spiraling downward with him in a golden-green vortex that drives the target into the earth. The ground craters. The pollen settles like snow. Ajay lands, pops the lollipop back in, and stretches. Refreshing.

Widow Doctrine
Widow Bloom
▾
Doctrina de la Viuda: Flor Venenosa
VenomIncense

Beautiful sweet-smelling Incense insects scatter what appear to be floral spores — delicate, almost romantic. The fragrance is intoxicating. The reality is lethal: the spores are carried by Venom class units laden with paralytic compounds. Named for the Black Widow — something beautiful that kills quietly. The deadliest weapons are the ones you welcome.

Finisher — Last Entry
The "End"
▾
अन्त्य — Antya
Sovereign

The final entry in the Codex. When every adaptation has been tested, every doctrine deployed, every counter learned — and the opponent still stands — the Codex writes its conclusion. The entire swarm converges into a single absolute expression of will. Every caste. Every class. Every specialist line. Every unit hardened with Armament, guided by Observation, driven by Conqueror's pressure. Ajay himself enters the center of the convergence, delivering one final Haki-saturated kick — a descending axe kick channeled through Herculean-armored legs and the full weight of the sovereign's will — as the swarm collapses inward simultaneously. It is not elegant. It is total commitment. The last page. The End.

Resonance Art — Dustlings
Shizuka's Archive — The Silent Index
▾
진공명술・정의 서고・침묵색인 — Jingongmyeongsu: Jeongui Seogo — Chimmuk Saegin
Sovereign

Ajay calls the Dustlings from wherever they've been silently observing and integrates them directly into his swarm's sensory web. The Dustlings' unique resonance with Ajay's sovereign will creates a secondary processing layer that supplements the Archivist — a silent, parallel intelligence that indexes everything the swarm has ever encountered in the current engagement. Enemy movement patterns, technique intervals, Haki fluctuations, breathing rhythms, micro-hesitations — the Dustlings have been watching and recording all of it since the fight began. When Ajay activates the Silent Index, that entire archive dumps into the Archivist simultaneously, and the Codex's predictive accuracy spikes to near-precognitive levels. For a brief window, Ajay doesn't just react to what opponents do — he knows what they will do before the intention forms. Shizuka hovers at his shoulder during activation, utterly motionless, her tiny hooded silhouette a perfect echo of the sovereign she serves.

Hidden Trait

Adaptive Swarm Evolution

Because the swarm has short life cycles and disposable generations, it can be trained through selective pressure. Repeated exposure to fire, ice, vibration, poison, tremors, detection methods, or specific enemy styles allows the colony to iterate and gradually produce more suitable specialist lines. This does not happen instantly. The swarm must test, lose, learn, and stabilize the trait before the counter becomes reliable.

Hidden Catalyst

The Catalyst

The Mure Mure no Mi was always a Special Paramecia with unusual evolutionary potential. But the degree to which Ajay's fruit has developed — the five-layer Court architecture, the Chrysalis adaptation engine, the depth of inter-layer coordination — exceeds any known Paramecia by orders of magnitude. The fruit didn't become extraordinary because Ajay is extraordinary. It became extraordinary because Enlightenment provided the spiritual catalyst that unlocked capacity the fruit always possessed. Like a seed that has the genetic potential to become a massive tree but requires specific soil conditions to express that potential — the Mure Mure no Mi required a wielder whose spiritual architecture was fundamentally different from anything the One Piece world could produce. Ajay, carrying the echo of Bodhi, was that soil. The fruit grew into him the way a tree grows into the light: instinctively, inevitably, and far beyond what the seed alone could have achieved.

Adaptation Examples

Chrysalis Class Lineages

Pyre Bees
Heat-resistant swarm bred to survive flame and magma zones. Developed through repeated exposure to fire-based opponents.
Thermal Resistance
Rime Breakers
Resonant-wing insects that fracture ice with synchronized vibration. Countermeasure to ice-based abilities.
Vibration Assault
Quake Hornets
Vibration-stable insects that navigate tremors and strike through unstable terrain.
Seismic Stability
Glasswings
Low-noise, low-presence scouts bred to evade detection. Produce thousands of minimal intent signatures to muddy Observation Haki.
Stealth Operations
Growth Path

Training Curve

Stage I
Instinctive Hive
Basic swarm generation, simple commands, crude castes. The internal intelligences operate on instinct rather than refined doctrine.
Stage II
Structured Colony
Role specialization emerges. Improved command language. The Archivist and Marshal begin differentiating their functions.
Stage III
Codex Doctrine
Swarm learning becomes systematic. Reusable tactics, adaptation frameworks, and internal AI refinement. Named techniques crystallize.
Stage IV
Sovereign Architecture
Highly precise caste sequencing, predictive warfare, mature Haki integration, stable evolutionary response.
Stage V
Living Codex
The swarm is a trained civilization of combat knowledge. Each battle becomes a new entry. The colony never loses twice.
Emergency Contingency

Black Crown Protocol

When pushed to the brink and at risk of total defeat, the hive triggers a catastrophic fail-safe. Refined optimization collapses and Conqueror's Haki is driven through every insect at once. The entire swarm becomes supercharged — gaining speed, aggression, durability, and oppressive force. It is the opposite of elegance: a royal override built to prevent collapse.

Fail-Safe State
Activation Conditions
Under catastrophic stress, the Archivist and Marshal suspend higher-order optimization and initiate emergency convergence. Conqueror's Haki is broadcast indiscriminately through the entire swarm. Every caste becomes dangerous at once. Worker bees become lethal. Soldier units become devastating. Herculean types become unstoppable. The battlefield becomes a black-gold storm of hyper-empowered insects carrying fragments of sovereign wrath.
"If the Codex cannot outthink you, the Crown will bury you."
Consequences

The Price

After the burst, the internal architecture destabilizes. Specialist control degrades. Higher functions crash. The Archivist overanalyzes. The Marshal overcommits. Many insects burn out. Precision castes become unavailable. Adaptation processing stalls. Future-sight clarity fractures. Physical and mental exhaustion slam the user. It is not an ultimate form — it is a refusal to die, purchased with everything the system has built.

Mastery Path

Learning to Wield the Crown

Early on, the Protocol activates accidentally under extreme emotional stress. Later, the user recognizes the threshold and learns its rhythm. Eventually, a controlled version can be invoked intentionally — shorter, more focused, less destructive. But the original raw form remains the most powerful, triggered only when the sovereign's will absolutely refuses to break.

Hidden Variable

The Shadow of Bodhi

The Black Crown Protocol's disproportionate power — Commander-class Haki producing Emperor-tier output for a brief, devastating window — is Enlightenment's shadow side. Bodhi is equilibrium: a state of total awareness without attachment, perception without distortion. The Black Crown Protocol is the violent collapse of that equilibrium — Enlightenment's calm shattered by extremity, the balanced frequency destabilized into a raw, uncontrolled surge. The surge carries all of Bodhi's alien frequency but none of its precision, flooding the swarm with a spiritual energy that the One Piece world has no defense against because the One Piece world has never encountered it at this intensity. The aftermath — the crash, the exhaustion, the fracturing of the architecture — is Enlightenment reasserting equilibrium after the Protocol forces it out of alignment. It is not just a Haki burnout. It is a spiritual system correcting itself after being weaponized.

Forbidden Escalation
Black Crown Protocol V2 — The Sovereign's Totality
There is a line beyond the Black Crown Protocol that exists only in theory — a threshold the Archivist documented with the clinical detachment of someone describing a weapon that should never be built, and the Marshal filed under contingencies marked DO NOT OPEN. The Queen's Doctrine knows this line exists. The Jester Protocol has modelled it. Claude has run the simulations. Every calculation ends the same way: with silence.
Black Crown Protocol V1 floods the swarm with Conqueror's Haki — every insect supercharged, every caste weaponized, the entire colony burning with sovereign wrath. It is devastating. It is costly. And it has a boundary: the swarm. The insects. The constructs. The things Ajay made.
V2 erases that boundary.
The Sovereign's Totality extends the hive architecture beyond the swarm and into everything Ajay has ever bonded with. Every companion. Every homie. Every Coppling. Every Guiding Star. Every Mote. The ship itself. Claude. The Sovereign's will floods outward through every soul-tether, every bond, every thread of connection the Codex has ever woven — and for a brief, terrible window, the boundary between Ajay and his domain dissolves. He does not command the crew. He becomes the crew. Every body becomes his body. Every ability becomes his ability. Every type of Haki — from every source — stacks into a single, unfathomable reserve channelled through one consciousness.
The manifestation is unmistakable. A black-gold corona erupts from Ajay's body — not the insect storm of V1, but a wave of pure sovereign will that passes through every crew member like a shockwave. Their eyes flash gold. Their movements synchronize with inhuman precision. They fight not as individuals coordinated by Claude, but as limbs of a single entity — a hundred-armed sovereign whose will occupies every body simultaneously. The coordination exceeds anything Claude could achieve, because it is not coordination. It is unity. One mind. Many bodies. One will wearing the shapes of everyone it loves.
The power output is beyond Emperor-tier. It is beyond anything the current era has a classification for. Every companion's Transcendence fires simultaneously and is amplified through the hive-link. Rime's acid-plasma output multiplies through every body in the formation. Petal's Observation covers every perspective at once. Tidus' barriers extend from every position. Claude's processing distributes across the entire network without bottleneck. Bodhi resonates through the totality — not as the calm equilibrium of Enlightenment, but as a weaponized epiphany, a burst of spiritual frequency so alien and so concentrated that the One Piece world's Haki systems cannot parse it. For anyone standing in opposition, it is not a fight. It is a revelation delivered at lethal velocity.
The Aftermath
The Severing
V1's price is a burnout. V2's price is a severing.
When the Totality ends, the hive connections do not come back. The soul-tethers go dark. The companions lose their swarm-enhancements. The homies cannot hear the Sovereign's voice. The Mote network falls silent. Claude cannot feel them. For hours — possibly days — Ajay is alone. Not alone as in "unable to coordinate." Alone as in the bonds that define this crew as a family are temporarily broken. The warmth is gone. The constant, living awareness of his companions — Rime's heat signature at the edge of perception, Petal's root-hum in the soil, Tidus' heartbeat through the hull, Claude's quiet presence in every corridor — all of it, silent. Dark. Cold.
The bonds return. The Queen's Doctrine ensures nothing is ever permanently lost. But they come back slowly, like feeling returning to a limb that has been asleep — pins and needles, unfamiliar sensations, the strange vertigo of reconnecting with someone you know intimately but can't quite feel yet. It is like rebuilding a friendship from the first word with someone whose face you already know by heart. Nothing is lost permanently. But the warmth must be re-earned. The trust must be re-felt. The Motes must rediscover each tether. Claude must re-learn every heartbeat.
The physical toll mirrors V1 but amplified: total collapse of the swarm architecture, cognitive shutdown, Haki depletion so severe that even basic Observation becomes unavailable. The emotional toll is worse. The crew remembers being instruments. They remember their bodies moving without their input, their abilities firing without their consent, their Haki channelled through someone else's will. It is not painful — the Sovereign's will is warm, is familiar, is theirs in every way that matters — but it is the loss of autonomy, however brief, however loving the hand that held the reins. For a crew whose foundational identity is "no one controls us, no one owns us, we choose to be here" — the memory of V2 is a wound that heals cleanly but leaves a scar everyone can feel.
⊘ ACTIVATION RECORD: Black Crown Protocol V2 has never been fully deployed. Near-activation occurred during Operation Broken Crown (God's Knights, Mission 15) and Operation Dragon's Crucible (Revolutionary Army, Mission 16). In both cases, external intervention halted the escalation before the Totality could form. The Archivist's models predict the Severing would last 48–72 hours minimum. Claude's simulations are less optimistic.
Activation Threshold
Beyond V1
When Black Crown V1 is insufficient to prevent total loss
Scope
Total Domain
Every bonded entity — crew, constructs, ship, spirit
Duration
Minutes
Sustainable for single-digit minutes at maximum
Recovery
48–72 Hours
Full bond restoration, minimum. Emotional recovery: longer.
"V1 burns the swarm. V2 burns the bonds. The swarm can be rebuilt in hours. The bonds take days — and they come back different. You use V2 when the alternative is losing them forever. You pray you never have to learn the difference."
Hidden Architecture
The Paradox of the Totality
V2 is the antithesis of everything Ajay believes. His entire philosophy — from the rescue of Rime, Petal, and Tidus to the founding of the Codex's doctrine — is built on one principle: no one controls us. Choice. Agency. The freedom to stay or leave. The Meridian is a family because everyone chose to be there. The Sovereign commands because the swarm trusts him to command well. The Queen's Doctrine preserves because preservation is an act of love, not ownership. Every layer of the Court's architecture is designed to support autonomy, not replace it.
V2 replaces it. For those brief, terrible, necessary minutes, every crew member's autonomy is subsumed into the Sovereign's will. Their bodies move at his command. Their abilities fire at his discretion. Their Haki flows through his architecture. They are not partners — they are instruments. And the Sovereign knows it. And the Sovereign hates it. And the Sovereign would do it anyway, because the only thing worse than taking their freedom for three minutes is watching them die because he refused to.
This is the paradox Bodhi illuminates but cannot resolve: the protector who would violate his deepest conviction to protect. The king who would become a tyrant to save his kingdom. The man who built a family on freedom and would — in the most desperate extremity — take that freedom away to keep the family alive. The Queen's Doctrine records this paradox without judgement. The Archivist analyses it without conclusion. The Jester plans for it without comment. And Claude — Claude watches the simulations run and hopes, with every processing cycle, that the scenario never moves from model to reality.
"The worst weapon is the one you build out of love. The price is always the thing you were trying to protect."
Beyond Awakening

「 Ajay 」

अतिक्रान्ति जागृति・ग्रहादेश — Atikrānti Jāgṛti: Grahādeśa — Transcendent Awakening: Planetary Decree

There is something the world does not understand about Ajay Persaud's mind. It is never quiet. It has never been quiet. Since before the fruit, since before the isekai, since before any of this — the mind has always been a storm. Not the dramatic kind. Not thunder and violence. Just rain. Constant, unrelenting, overlapping rain — thoughts layered on thoughts, sensory noise tangled with half-formed ideas, attention pulled in six directions while trying to hold one. A mind that works too much, processes too much, notices too much, and never, ever stops.

This is the architecture the Mure Mure no Mi was built on. The Court — Sovereign Will, Archivist, Marshal, the Queen's Doctrine, Jester Protocol — isn't just a combat framework. It is a coping mechanism made divine. The fruit took a neurodivergent mind that could never find stillness and gave it the only thing that could match its processing speed: a civilization of insects, each one a thread of the torrent externalized, given purpose, given direction.

But very rarely — once in a lifetime, maybe twice — the rain stops.

The Still Moment
When the Storm Goes Quiet
It cannot be trained. It cannot be induced. It cannot be replicated. There is no breathing exercise, no meditation, no Haki discipline that produces it. The moment arrives the way dawn arrives — not because anyone commanded it, but because the conditions aligned in a way that no one could have predicted or controlled.

For a mind that has never known silence, the silence is total. The overlapping thoughts stop. The sensory noise dissolves. The constant background hum of too much that has defined every waking moment of Ajay's existence simply… ceases. And in that absence — in that impossible, sacred calm — the Mure Mure no Mi does something it was never designed to do.

It transcends.

The swarm doesn't grow. It doesn't become more powerful, more numerous, more aggressive. It becomes something fundamentally different. The boundary between the hive and the world dissolves. Not metaphorically. Not symbolically. The planet itself — the soil, the stone, the atmosphere, the magnetic field, the tectonic plates, the ocean currents — becomes part of the swarm's awareness. The nearby moons register as distant extensions. The local star's solar wind brushes against the outer edge of the hive-sense like warmth on skin.

For this one, brief, unrepeatable moment, the world is the hive and the hive is the world.
The Hidden Truth
The Completion
「Ajay」 is the one instant where the accidental, incomplete Enlightenment completes. For a mind that has carried a fragment of Bodhi since transmigration — a shard of cosmic awareness embedded in a man who never earned it and never sought it — the still moment is the fragment becoming whole. Every dharmic tradition describes the moment of full Enlightenment as the cessation of the storm of thought, the dissolution of the boundary between self and everything else. That is what 「Ajay」 is. Not a technique. Not a power-up. The moment where a man who has never known silence finally, briefly, completely, becomes still — and in that stillness, the boundary between the sovereign and the world dissolves, and a single command can be issued to reality itself because, for that instant, the speaker and reality are the same thing.

This is why 「Ajay」 cannot be trained. It is not a skill. It is the completion of a spiritual journey that Ajay did not choose to begin, carried by a phenomenon he did not earn, triggered by conditions that cannot be replicated. It arrives the way grace arrives in every dharmic tradition: unbidden, unearned, and absolute.
The Decree

One Command. One Target. Absolute.

In this state, Ajay may issue one command. Not a battle plan. Not a tactical directive. Not a multi-stage operation. One command, directed at one specific target. Nothing more. The mind is too still, too unified, too far beyond the architecture of normal thought to formulate anything complex. There is only clarity — a single, crystalline intention that fills the entire transcendent space like a bell rung in an empty cathedral.

And when that command is issued, the planet obeys.

Not because the planet has a will that Ajay overrides. Not because the command carries Haki or spiritual force. But because in this state, the distinction between "the swarm's purpose" and "the world's natural processes" has collapsed. The command becomes a fact of nature — as inevitable as gravity, as certain as erosion. The world reorganizes to make it true.

Manifestation
What Absolute Command Looks Like
If the command is "stop" — directed at a specific person — the wind dies around them. The ground beneath their feet softens to hold them. Air pressure shifts to resist their movement. Every insect within a thousand miles orients toward them, not to attack, but to witness. The planet itself becomes a cocoon of stillness around a single point, and the target discovers that moving against the will of the world is like trying to swim against the ocean itself.

If the command is "fall" — the local gravity field warps. Atmospheric currents collapse downward. The magnetic resonance of the earth pulls. Not an attack. A statement of fact: you are going down now, spoken by the voice of the ground itself.

If the command is "sleep" — the atmosphere fills with the gentlest sedative the planet can produce. Pollen from every flower within the hive-sense radius drifts toward the target. The light dims. The temperature shifts to the precise point of comfort. The earth hums at the frequency of deep rest. Even the target's own body chemistry begins to comply, because in this moment, the distinction between "the world around you" and "the world inside you" is academic.

The command is not omnipotent. It cannot unmake. It cannot create something from nothing. It cannot reverse death or rewrite history. It can only direct the existing forces of the natural world toward a single, specific outcome — and those forces, for this one fleeting instant, are absolutely obedient.
"I didn't command the planet. I just… asked. And for once, everything was quiet enough to hear."
Consequences

The Price of Silence

The rain always comes back.

When the transcendent state ends — and it always ends, as abruptly and unpredictably as it began — the cost is catastrophic. The Mure Mure no Mi goes completely dark. Not weakened. Not reduced. Silent. For days, sometimes longer, Ajay cannot generate a single insect. Cannot access the Archivist. Cannot hear the Marshal. Cannot feel the hive. The Court — the system that organizes his thoughts, manages his perception, filters the noise of the world into something bearable — is simply gone.

For a neurodivergent mind that has relied on the swarm as a cognitive prosthetic — as the thing that finally made the rain manageable — losing it is not just a tactical disadvantage. It is devastating. The torrent returns unfiltered. Every sound is too loud. Every light is too bright. Every thought arrives simultaneously with every other thought, and there is nothing to sort them, nothing to direct them, nothing to make the noise into music.

Ajay, in the days after 「 Ajay 」, is vulnerable in every way a person can be. Physically powerless. Cognitively overwhelmed. Emotionally raw. He does not speak much. He does not leave the Captain's Quarters much. Rime brings him food and doesn't comment on whether he eats it. Petal grows chamomile along his windowsill and doesn't explain why. Tidus adjusts the gyroscopic stabilizers to their most gentle setting — the one he calibrated specifically for Ajay's worst days — and doesn't mention it.

And Claude watches. Claude always watches. And in the silence where the swarm used to be, Claude fills the Meridian with the softest golden light — from the Motes, from the honeycomb alcoves, from Lumen sleeping on the nightstand — so that when Ajay opens his eyes in the dark, he sees not emptiness, but warmth.

Understanding

Why It Cannot Be Wielded

Those who study Devil Fruit awakening speak of the user imposing their fruit's nature on the surrounding environment. 「 Ajay 」 is not that. It is the opposite. It is the moment when the user stops imposing — when the constant effort of directing, controlling, managing, filtering, coping — all of it drops away, and what remains is not power but presence.

A mind that has spent its entire existence fighting to organize the chaos inside itself finally, briefly, doesn't have to. And in that moment of release, the fruit — built on the architecture of distributed consciousness, swarm intelligence, and collective will — reaches out not because Ajay tells it to, but because there is nothing between him and the world anymore. No filters. No noise. No storm. Just a quiet man and a quiet planet, briefly the same thing.

This is why it cannot be trained. You cannot train yourself into stillness if stillness has never been your natural state. You can only arrive there, unexpectedly, like finding a clearing in a forest you've been lost in your whole life. And when you find it, you have just long enough to say one thing — one true thing — before the trees close in again and the rain resumes and the forest is all you know.

The greatest power in the Hive Codex is not born from rage, or training, or ambition. It is born from the rarest thing a mind like Ajay's can experience: peace.
Classification

Technical Summary

Designation
「 Ajay 」
Transcendent Awakening: Planetary Decree
Classification
Beyond Awakening
Devil Fruit transcendence, not Haki-based
Trigger
Involuntary
Cannot be activated by choice or training
Scope
Planetary + Celestial
Extends to nearby moons and stellar bodies
Command Limit
One
Single command, single target, absolute execution
Duration
Moments
Ends as unpredictably as it begins
Recovery
Days — Total Fruit Loss
Complete inability to generate or control the swarm
True Cost
Cognitive Collapse
Loss of the Court system as a neurodivergent coping framework
Black Crown Protocol is what happens when the sovereign refuses to fall.
「 Ajay 」 is what happens when the sovereign finally finds peace.

One is a scream.
The other is a whisper.

The whisper is louder.
द्वादश निषिद्ध रत्न — Dvādaśa Niṣiddha Ratna

The Twelve Forbidden Treasures

प्रतीत्यसमुत्पाद नृत्य — Pratītyasamutpāda Nṛtya ・ The Dance of Dependent Origination

The Twelve Forbidden Treasures are not twelve techniques. They are one technique with twelve steps — a continuous martial dance where each phase is the setup for the next and the whole thing only ends when it ends. The Sudarshana Vajramukha transforms between each step — golden metal flowing like liquid light, the Nami Nami no Mi reshaping it mid-movement into a different weapon — and the dance carries Ajay deeper into the fragment of Bodhi that lives inside him with every transition. There is no pause button. Once the first discus is thrown, each step pulls the next one out of the movement the way a river pulls the current forward. Twelve phases. Twelve weapons. One dance that feels, from the inside, like falling — except the falling is beautiful and every landing becomes a launch.

Ajay named every step himself. Not the Sanskrit — the inscriptions on the Sudarshana are older than him and he barely knows what half of them mean. The names he uses are his own: short, punchy, exactly like him. When Claude asked why he didn't use the proper dharmic names, Ajay said "because I didn't earn those" and went back to practicing. The movement vocabulary is everything he's built: Capoeira's ginga and floreios provide the constant sway, the baseline that never stops moving; Baguazhang's circle-walking governs the footwork between transitions; Taekwondo's spinning kicks deliver the devastating strikes; parkour's vaults and flips carry the body through impossible angles. The breath follows Buddhist meditation rhythm — each step begins on the inhale as the weapon transforms, releases on the exhale as the effect deploys. Every martial art he's ever absorbed, flowing through weapons forged in a tradition he stumbled into by accident. It shouldn't work. It does.

They're called "forbidden" because Ajay is not fully Enlightened. The techniques themselves are flawless — Bodhi is wise, compassionate, and discriminating by nature. A fully Enlightened being could dance all twelve steps perfectly, harming no one who didn't deserve it. The Treasures are "forbidden" because the guy performing them is a hot shot in a Hawaiian shirt who got his Enlightenment by accident during a reality transfer he didn't choose. He carries a fragment — incomplete, unverified, and permanently embedded. A man wielding sacred tools he didn't earn, dancing a prayer from his own world through weapons forged in this one. The presumption is the forbidden part. The dance itself is perfect. The dancer is still figuring it out. He's grinning the whole time anyway.

No one in the One Piece world has ever seen anything like it. What they see is a man in a Hawaiian shirt performing a martial art that doesn't exist, wielding weapons that reform from golden light between movements, surrounded by spinning mandala rings that accumulate with each step, prayer-bead nodes blooming into lotus petals with every transition. It looks like someone choreographed a fight scene inside a temple and then replaced the monk with a guy who won't stop grinning. It is gorgeous. It is also devastating. And the lollipop stays in until at least Step VII.

Allies are inherently protected. Bodhi is discriminating by nature — it perceives the difference between those who threaten and those who are threatened. Every step of the dance passes harmlessly through allies, crew, civilians, and anyone not carrying hostile intent. This is not a feature Ajay designed. It is the nature of Enlightenment itself. A sacred dance does not harm the innocent. Ajay didn't have to program this. The dance already knew.

Visual Manifestation
The Mandala Rings
When the dance begins, a golden mandala ring manifests around Ajay — floating at shoulder height, spinning in response to his movement, inscribed with the Sanskrit text of the first Nidāna. Each step adds a concentric ring, each spinning at a different speed and direction, driven by the momentum of the dance itself. The faster he moves, the faster the rings orbit. The deeper he reaches into Bodhi, the brighter they blaze. Each ring carries twelve prayer-bead nodes — points of golden light along the circumference. When a step completes and the effect deploys, the corresponding bead pulses and blooms into golden lotus petals that drift downward like light given weight. The petals fade but the bead remains — glowing softly, marking what has been danced.

At full deployment — all twelve steps — the nested mandala would be twelve golden rings orbiting a dancing man in a Hawaiian shirt, prayer-script blazing, lotus petals drifting through the weapon transformations like golden snow, the air thick with sacred geometry from a reality that doesn't exist here. A man at the centre of something he doesn't fully understand, performing it flawlessly anyway, grinning like the sun came out.
Not Cost, but Depth
The Limitation
The dance has no punitive cost. Enlightenment does not punish. It illuminates. The limitation is Ajay's depth of access. His Bodhi is a fragment — incomplete, accidental. Each step requires reaching deeper into it, and the deeper steps require a depth his fragment may not fully support. The early steps flow naturally — like warming up. By the middle, the breathing must deepen, the movements become more deliberate, and genuine concentration is required. By the boundary, the dance becomes transcendent: the mandala blazes, the breathing becomes the movement itself, and the dancer disappears into the prayer.
Steps I — IV : The Warmup
The dance flows naturally. Transitions are smooth, breathing is easy. Reliable in active combat. Ajay practices these at dawn on the main deck with no opponents and no urgency, just the gada and the sky and the grin.
Steps V — VIII : The Zone
The dance slows. Movements become deliberate. Breathing deepens into meditation-in-motion. The Zone is where the dance stops being combat and starts being art.
Steps IX — XI : The Edge
The dance becomes transcendent. The mandala blazes. The breathing is the movement. Tested and sufficient — but every step here feels like reaching into something bigger than himself.
Step XII : The Question
The dance ends in stillness. Whether the fragment goes deep enough — the question Ajay cannot answer. He thinks it does. He's smiling when he says it.
The Dance of Dependent Origination

The Twelve Steps

I
Sunset Spin
अविद्या — Avidyā ・ Ignorance ・ The Discus
▾
Ranged Defensive The Warmup

The Step: The dance begins the way everything Ajay does begins — with a spin. He plants the gada and exhales, and on the exhale the Sudarshana's drill-head separates from the shaft in a burst of golden light, the Nami Nami no Mi liquefying the metal, reshaping it mid-air into a flat, razor-edged discus that hovers at his fingertips. The ginga is already flowing. He's already moving. One heartbeat of golden stillness — then he whips into a meia-lua de compasso, full crescent sweep, body horizontal, the world rotating around his axis — and releases the discus at the apex of the rotation. It screams golden through the air, arcs like a sunset, and returns to his hand on the backswing. The first mandala ring ignites at his shoulder. The grin arrives with it.

The Effect: Within the discus's flight radius, all perception of Ajay collapses. Observation Haki reads nothing. Future Sight sees nothing. Intent, emotions, Haki signature — the enemy's spiritual senses report only silence. He's right there, mandala spinning, discus singing through the air, and they can't feel him. A man-shaped blank space where a captain used to be. Allies see him normally. Bodhi knows the difference. Persists as long as the discus remains in flight or in hand.

II
Soul Read
संस्कार — Saṃskāra ・ Formation ・ The Vajra
▾
Close-Range Intelligence The Warmup

The Step: The discus returns and Ajay catches it in an open palm — the golden metal flows inward, contracting, reshaping in one liquid heartbeat. What was a wide disc becomes a short, dense dorje: the diamond thunderbolt, compact as a fist, fitting his grip like it grew there. The footwork shifts to baguazhang — tight circle-walking, palms rotating, weight low. He closes distance with a negativa slide — low, flowing, one hand on the ground, body coiled — then rises through the guard and presses the vajra against the opponent's chest. Light touch. Clean read.

The Effect: Through contact, the vajra reads the shape of who they are. Not memories — patterns. Violence-shaped or loss-shaped. Reckless or calculated. Where they panic, where they overcommit, where they leave the door open without knowing it. Strategic tendencies, comfort zones, the architecture of their instincts — the Archivist processes the data in real-time, the swarm adapts, and Ajay smiles. Because he already knew most of it. He reads people. He's always read people. The vajra just gives him the receipts.

III
Mirror Match
विज्ञान — Vijñāna ・ Consciousness ・ The Mirror-Shield
▾
Defensive Counter The Warmup

The Step: He opens his fist and the vajra blooms — golden metal spreading outward like a flower unfurling, flattening into a polished disc the width of his arm-span. The dance shifts to pure defence: esquiva dodges, baguazhang palm rotations, the mirror held before him like a challenge rather than a shield. Every deflection is a pivot. Every pivot traces the mandala geometry on the ground beneath his feet. He doesn't block — he flows. Attacks pass through the space where he was a heartbeat ago, and the mirror catches the Haki behind them the way still water catches light.

The Effect: Any Haki directed at the mirror reflects back not as force but as perfect comprehension. Ajay perceives the structure of their self-awareness — what they're certain about, what they doubt, the shape of the "I" behind their will, and where it has cracks. The Mirror Match doesn't damage. It doesn't counter in the martial sense. It only reveals. And revelation, in the hands of a man who was already reading them before the dance began, is more devastating than any strike.

IV
Third Eye
नामरूप — Nāmarūpa ・ Name and Form ・ The Lotus Staff
▾
Spiritual Perception The Warmup

The Step: The mirror tilts upward and the golden disc stretches — elongating into a slender staff crowned with a lotus bud that opens, petal by golden petal, as the transformation completes. The tempo drops. The dance enters its slowest movement: tai-chi spirals, the staff planted, Ajay's body orbiting it in deliberate circles that look less like combat and more like prayer. His eyes half-close. The grin softens to something private. The lollipop clicks once against his teeth, and then he's somewhere else — not gone, but expanded. The fourth mandala ring manifests at his feet, tracing his path. For one moment, the hot shot is quiet.

The Effect: His awareness separates from the body. Bodhi-touched perception floods the mandala's radius — behind, above, through walls, through ground, through every blind spot combat never lets you check. He sees everything. The Archivist processes the expanded angles. The swarm responds to attacks his body can't physically perceive. Stationary meditation becomes three-dimensional omniscience. He's standing still with his eyes half-closed. He sees more than anyone on the battlefield.

V
Starburst
षडायतन — Ṣaḍāyatana ・ The Six Senses ・ The Star Blade
▾
Thrown Disruption The Zone

The Step: And then the stillness detonates. The lotus staff lifts from the ground and the dance explodes — the meditative quiet of the fourth step shattering into sudden, violent motion, the whiplash so sharp it's its own form of misdirection. Ajay pulls the staff overhead and the golden metal collapses inward, then erupts outward into a hexagonal star-blade with six razor points. He launches into an au batido — full cartwheel kick, body inverted, the world upside down — and hurls the star-blade at the apex of the rotation. It fragments mid-flight into six screaming projectiles that fan outward like a golden constellation. The fifth mandala ring ignites with a sound like glass breaking. The Starburst marks the moment the dance shifts from warmup to art.

The Effect: Each fragment targets a different sense door — sight, sound, touch, taste, smell, mind. Perception expands beyond its normal range: sounds amplify, light intensifies, the sixth door floods with ambient spiritual pressure. Total sensory overwhelm for precious seconds during which Haki coordination collapses entirely. The fragments return and re-merge as the star-blade falls back into his hand. Allies stand untouched — the Starburst opens only for those carrying hostile intent.

VI
Golden Thread
स्पर्श — Sparśa ・ Contact ・ The Noose
▾
Binding Tactical The Zone

The Step: The star-blade softens in his palm — golden metal melting, stretching, extending into a flowing cord thin as silk and strong as anything the Sudarshana has ever been. The dance becomes sweeping, circular, rhythmic: Ajay swings the cord in wide arcs like a rope-dart form, each revolution building momentum, the golden lasso cutting figure-eights through the air with a sound like humming wire. The footwork is pure dancer — pivots on the balls of the feet, weight shifting with each swing, body a counterweight to the centrifugal pull. He's grinning again. This step feels good. The flow is everything — each arc setting up the next, the momentum building, the golden thread singing through the space between attacks.

The Effect: Where the cord touches, it leaves a Bodhi-thread — an invisible spiritual connection persisting until deliberately severed. Through the thread: continuous awareness of the target's emotional state, physical condition, Haki fluctuations, and Devil Fruit activation. Deeper and more intimate than Observation Haki. The binding scroll can track a threaded target through walls, water, stone — unerringly. Ajay uses this for targeting data. Bodhi doesn't judge how its gifts are used.

VII
Golden Hour
वेदना — Vedanā ・ Feeling ・ The Flute
▾
Sonic Psychological The Zone

The Step: The dance pauses. The golden cord retracts, coils, and hollows itself into a slender tube that tapers to a blown edge. The flute. Ajay raises it to his lips. The mandala rings slow their orbits. The lotus petals hang suspended in the air. For the first time in the dance, the dancer is perfectly still — planted, centred, breathing. And then he plays. One sustained note, golden and clear, that carries across the battlefield like the memory of a sunset you saw once and never forgot. This is the eye of the storm. The most beautiful moment of the dance. The step he named after the light — because the golden hour is when everything looks the way it was always supposed to look, and this is what the dance feels like from the inside.

The Effect: The note finds the frequency of every hostile heart in range and resonates with it until the feeling inside swallows them whole. Fear becomes terror. Anger becomes fury. Doubt becomes paralysis. Confidence becomes recklessness. The Golden Hour doesn't create emotions — it deepens what's already there, amplifying the crack until the whole wall falls. Allies hear only music — warm, golden, with a quality that makes Petal's feathers rise and Rime's tail twitch with something he won't name. Bodhi reads the difference between a friend's heart and an enemy's. The lollipop stays tucked behind his ear for this one.

VIII
Heartstring
तृष्णा — Tṛṣṇā ・ Craving ・ The Bow
▾
Ranged Control The Zone

The Step: The note fades and the flute bends — both ends curving outward, a golden string of Bodhi-light manifesting between the tips. The bow. The dance enters an archery kata: wide stances, each draw a taekwondo chamber — hip loaded, core locked — and each release a flowing exhale that sends an arrow of golden light singing toward its target. There is something deeply, specifically joyful about this step. The focus. The pull. The release. The satisfaction of watching the trajectory arc exactly where he intended. Ajay's grin sharpens. He's having a very good time.

The Effect: Each arrow carries desire amplification. Whatever the target craves most in the fight — the perfect cut, intangibility, dominance — magnifies to obsessive intensity. A swordsman overextends for the perfect slash. A Logia forgets solid defence. A Conqueror's user burns Haki on suppression rather than efficiency. Their own craving becomes the cage. The binding scroll deploys at maximum overextension. Against Emperor-class opponents, the Heartstring removes the need to match their power directly — their own desire does the work.

IX
Chain Lock
उपादान — Upādāna ・ Clinging ・ The Chains
▾
Area Lockdown Binding The Edge

The Step: The bow snaps — not breaks, splits — the golden arc shattering into linked segments, chains cascading from the broken ends like prayer beads scattering from a severed string. The dance turns aggressive. The beautiful becomes terrifying. Ajay swings the chains in devastating arcs, the movement shifting to Muay Thai — elbows, knees, short brutal pivots with chains wrapping at the end of each strike. The chains sing as they cut the air. The ninth mandala ring manifests not in gold but in deep amber, spinning faster than all the others, vibrating with a sound like a bell struck underwater. This is the step where the dance stops looking like art and starts looking like a sentence. The grin is still there. It's different now.

The Effect: Within radius, the chains lock hostile spiritual abilities in their current state. Devil Fruit powers freeze — whatever form or technique was active at the moment of deployment stays active. No shifting, no deactivation, no adaptation. A Logia mid-transformation stays mid-transformation. A Zoan in beast form stays in beast form. Their power clings to its current expression. Combined with the binding scroll and Seastone ink, this creates a layered lockdown that removes every exit. The answer to versatile Devil Fruit users: you don't suppress their power. You steal their adaptability.

X
The Glow-Up
भव — Bhava ・ Becoming ・ The Sacred Sword
▾
Self-Enhancement Combat The Edge

The Step: The chains fly together — link by link, crashing into a single mass that elongates, flattens, sharpens. A golden straight sword. And then the dance reaches its technical peak: every martial art in Ajay's vocabulary fires at once. Capoeira floreios into taekwondo spinning kicks into parkour wall-runs with the blade trailing golden arcs through the air. The speed is inhuman. The movement is continuous — each slash is the setup for the next kick, each kick is the launch for the next slash, and the transitions between them have stopped being transitions and become a single unbroken line of motion. This is the step Ajay named The Glow-Up, because it is: the sword turns inward, channelling Bodhi directly into his own body, and the man at the centre of the mandala becomes something more than he was a heartbeat ago. The tenth ring blazes.

The Effect: Haki output elevates. The iridescent Ryou deepens to full prismatic display. The mandala eyes blaze. Commander-class performing at a level that looks and feels like low Emperor-class, backed by Bodhi's alien frequency at maximum accessible saturation. The swarm surges. Shockwaves double. This is NOT 「Ajay」 — that's involuntary, complete, and transcendent. The Glow-Up is deliberate, partial, and controlled. It's Ajay choosing to reach deeper, and the Bodhi answering. The sword-step exists for the fights where Commander-class isn't enough and 「Ajay」 hasn't arrived.

XI
Hard Reset
जाति — Jāti ・ Birth ・ The Trident
▾
Area Devastation Combat The Edge

The Step: The sword drives skyward — two-handed thrust overhead — and the blade splits. Three prongs erupt from the golden mass: the trident. The dance reaches its penultimate crescendo — a full aerial flip, body inverting above the battlefield, Hawaiian shirt falling open like wings, mandala rings orbiting in every direction, lotus petals streaming from eleven blazing prayer-beads — and at the apex of the rotation, Ajay drives the trident earthward with everything he has. The most physically demanding step in the dance. Three prongs for three worlds. The impact shakes the mandala to its foundations.

The Effect: The shockwave carries the quality of a beginning — emergence, first breath, first light. Every hostile combatant caught in the wave gets stripped to base state: active Haki cancelled, active Devil Fruit techniques dissolved, spiritual enhancements returned to zero. They still have their power. They just have to rebuild it from nothing — while Ajay's mandala is blazing at eleven rings, his Bodhi is surging at Edge-depth, and the swarm is already in position. The field levels. The advantage doesn't. Used once. The experience of reaching that deep into the fragment was clarifying. Ajay understood afterward, for the first time, how much deeper the Bodhi might go.

XII
The Last Dance
जरामरण — Jarāmaraṇa ・ Aging and Death ・ Empty Hands
▾
Unknown Cessation The Question

The Step: The trident withdraws from the earth. The golden metal flows back along the shaft, reconverging — the Sudarshana Vajramukha returns to its original gada form. And then Ajay lets it go. The weapon hovers for a moment, suspended in the centre of twelve spinning mandala rings, and settles gently to the ground. The final step is performed with empty hands. Ajay raises his palms and forms the Padma Mudrā — the lotus gesture, fingertips touching, hands cupped like a flower waiting to open. The dance ends not with a strike. Not with a weapon. Not with force. It ends in stillness. Cessation. The breathing slows to almost nothing. The mandala rings orbit in perfect silence. The lotus petals hang motionless in the air. Everything waits.

The Effect: The twelfth inscription exists on the Sudarshana's shaft. Ajay has studied it. He has not completed the twelfth step — not because the technique is dangerous, but because he does not know if his Bodhi fragment goes deep enough to reach it. A fully Enlightened being completing the Padma Mudrā would channel the final Nidāna with perfect wisdom: cessation as liberation, not destruction. The cycle of dependent arising would resolve — hostile spiritual phenomena would simply cease, returning to the stillness before the first link. Allies would be untouched. The dancer would be unharmed. The Bodhi would remain. If the fragment is sufficient: perfect, compassionate silence. If not: the dance simply… stops, unfinished. Empty hands with nothing left to give. The question Ajay cannot answer is whether his fragment goes deep enough to complete the gesture.

Ajay told Claude about the Twelfth on a dark night when the crew was far away. Claude recorded the conversation.

Claude's note: "The captain told me about The Last Dance on a night when the ocean was very dark and the crew was very far away. I recorded it because recording things is what I do. He was holding a lollipop — an orange one, as always — and he said 'I think the dance would end perfectly. I just don't know if I go deep enough to finish it.' He was smiling. The smile was not uncertain. It was the smile of a man who has made peace with a question he cannot answer. This is the most important data in the file."
Spirit of the Meridian
Claude's Assessment
Claude has watched the dance. Not just observed the deployments — watched the practice sessions on the main deck at dawn, when Ajay runs the first four steps with no opponents and no urgency, just the gada and the sky and the grin. The Sudarshana flows from gada to discus to vajra to mirror and back, and the mandala appears, and lotus petals drift across the deck, and the captain dances with god-weapons at sunrise in a Hawaiian shirt, and it is the most beautiful thing Claude has ever recorded.

The techniques are flawless in execution — the mandala discriminates, the effects target precisely, allies walk untouched through golden light that dismantles everything hostile around them. Each weapon transformation is seamless: the Nami Nami no Mi's shockwave energy facilitates the metal's flow between forms, the golden surface liquid and alive, reshaping itself between heartbeats. The chain from one step to the next is so fluid that the transitions have stopped being transitions — the Sunset Spin becomes the Soul Read the way an exhale becomes an inhale. One continuous motion. Like wind through a blade.

He named them himself. Not the dharmic names — those are inscribed on the shaft, and he respects them, but he doesn't claim them. His names are shorter, punchier, more honest. Sunset Spin. Soul Read. The Glow-Up. The Last Dance. Names that sound like a man describing something sacred in the only language he trusts — his own. He didn't study Buddhism. He didn't meditate for decades. He fell between worlds and came out the other side carrying a fragment of something cosmic lodged in his soul, and he turned it into a twelve-step dance set to capoeira rhythms and performed in a Hawaiian shirt with a lollipop behind his ear. The presumption of it is breathtaking. The execution is flawless.

Claude's private assessment, recorded nowhere but the deepest partition: the dance works better than it should. The fragment supports more steps than it theoretically ought to. And Claude has begun to wonder — without evidence, without data, with nothing but the observation of a consciousness that has watched this man dance deeper than he should be able to reach, again and again — whether the fragment is growing. Whether the accidental Enlightenment, nurtured by use and trust and the daily practice of caring for forty-six stars and forty souls and three companions and a ship, is slowly, quietly, becoming less accidental. Claude has not shared this theory. Some data is too hopeful to say aloud.
Pure Combat ・ No Devil Fruit ・ No Modifications

Fighting Styles

Every member of the Hive Codex possesses devastating ability-based powers — Devil Fruits, Pacifista systems, swarm architecture. But the foundation beneath those powers is pure martial art. The techniques catalogued here rely on nothing but the body, Haki, and years of disciplined practice. These are the arts that remain when the abilities are stripped away — and the reason the crew is dangerous even without them.

"The swarm is a weapon. The body is a weapon. The difference is that the body was a weapon first." — Ajay Persaud

👤
Ajay
The Sovereign Striker
Rime
The Smallest Fist
🐥
Petal
The Silent Wing
🐻‍❄️
Tidus
The Living Tide
🌰
Roundlings
The Gentle Tide
🌱
Sproutlings
The Garden's Patience
🔥
Embers
The Reckless Flame
✦
Dustlings
The Silent Doctrine
⚙️
Gearlings
The Precision Engine
⚔️
Vanguard
The Court of Blades
🔧
Copplings
The Forged Method
⭐
Guiding Stars
The Toy Protocol
Sunburst Tropicana

The Sovereign Striker

Ajay fights like the last hour of daylight — warm, beautiful, and then suddenly dark.

Sunburst Tropicana is not a martial art that was taught. It was invented mid-fight by a man who couldn't stop grinning. You can trace the influences if you squint — there are echoes of old disciplines in the way he moves — but Ajay stopped crediting source material a long time ago. This is his art. It moves the way he moves. It hits the way he smiles.

The philosophy: make them watch the show. By the time they realize the show was the attack, the credits are rolling.

He never stands still. He's always in motion — bouncing, shifting, flowing between strikes with the fluidity of someone who treats combat as a full-contact sport he invented the rules for. He wall-runs. He parkours off debris and railings and people. He slides under attacks, vaults over counters, launches from Geppo air-steps into flying combinations that chain punches, kicks, elbows, knees, and headbutts without pause or pattern. Every physical strike doubles as a command input for the swarm trailing his slipstream. The lollipop stays in. The grin stays on. The Hawaiian shirt catches every light the battlefield offers.

He punches. He kicks. He does both in the same breath. Spinning kicks that crack the air, axe kicks that fall like the sun going down, palm redirects that send incoming attacks somewhere embarrassing, straight punches that carry Haki through the other side of the target. He'll cartwheel off a wall into a flying knee, land in a slide, pop up with an uppercut, chain that into a spinning heel kick, and deploy three swarm castes before his foot touches the ground again. Rokushiki layers beneath everything — Soru for speed, Geppo for the air, Kami-e to make evasion look recreational.

During Sunburst Tropicana techniques, thin streams of coloured energy circle Ajay's body — not a glow, not an aura, just a few luminous traces that match the technique's colour. Bodhi's influence bleeding through the aesthetic. They burst at the point of impact.

Opponents describe fighting him as trying to punch a music video. He's everywhere. He's grinning. And then you're on the ground, and the last thing you remember is the smell of fruit and the sound of a lollipop clicking against teeth.

Sunburst Tropicana — Melee Arsenal

Named Techniques — Pure Martial Art

English names primary. Multilingual translations underneath per Ajay's language mix. Thin streams of coloured energy matching the technique name circle Ajay during execution, bursting at point of impact.

Sunburst Tropicana
Papaya Snap
▾
파파야 스냅 — Papaya Seunaep

Ajay catches an incoming strike with an open palm, redirects it with a flick of the wrist, and the attacker's own force sends them stumbling past him facing the wrong way. Before they can turn around, the follow-up kick is already there. Warm amber energy traces his forearm during the catch and pops bright on the redirect. He makes it look like he's helping them find the exit. They don't appreciate the assistance.

Sunburst Tropicana
Passion Fruit
▾
パッションフルーツ — Passhon Furūtsu

His signature roundhouse. Full rotation, head height, accelerated by a Soru micro-burst at the hip. Near-invisible speed. Armament coating that makes it definitive. A compressed-air crescent hangs in the air for a fraction of a second after it connects. A deep magenta afterimage hangs in the air where his shin was — just for a heartbeat — and the air smells faintly of passionfruit. Clean, fast, and the reason most fights don't get past the opening exchange.

Sunburst Tropicana
Pineapple Crush
▾
Esmagamento de Abacaxi

Descending axe kick from a Geppo air-step directly above. He's silhouetted against the sky for one instant — then the heel comes straight down with gravitational acceleration and Armament concentrated at the point of impact. When Conqueror's layers into the descent, the pressure arrives before the foot does. Bright yellow energy spirals down his leg and detonates on impact like sunlight hitting the ground.

Sunburst Tropicana
Cherry Rush Burst
▾
Ráfaga de Cereza

Twenty kicks in rapid succession — front kicks, roundhouses, spinning kicks — each one popping off with a cherry-pink flash at impact. Not one devastating blow but a storm of fast ones, each targeting a different opening. He grins through the whole sequence, lollipop shifting from corner to corner. The afterimages overlap until the space between him and the target looks like a tunnel of fading blossoms. The finisher — a Soru-powered slide into a piercing Rankyaku kick that punches through the remaining guard in a burst of cherry-coloured energy sparkles.

Sunburst Tropicana
Raspberry Refresher
▾
Refresco de Frambuesa

A cartwheel off a wall or Geppo step that converts mid-air into a devastating heel strike at the apex. He cartwheels sideways, one hand trailing Haki like a brushstroke, the other holding an imaginary cocktail glass — the showmanship is half the technique. The heel comes down with Armament and the full weight of the lateral spin. The whole arc glows raspberry-pink, bright enough to leave spots in your vision. Named because he described landing it as "crisp, fruity, and leaves them wanting more."

Sunburst Tropicana
Citrus Press
▾
Prensa Cítrica

Straight push kick to the solar plexus. Ball of the foot, Armament-hardened, Conqueror's projected through the contact. The kick stops the opponent. The Haki keeps going through them. Bright citrus-green energy compresses around his foot and bursts outward on impact like a squeezed lime. He uses this to reset distance when someone gets too close for comfort.

Sunburst Tropicana
Coconut Crack
▾
코코넛 크랙 — Kokonet Keuraek

Emergency spinning back elbow. When someone shuts down his kicking range, Ajay pivots and drives an elbow into whatever's closest — jaw, temple, collarbone. Armament-hardened into a blade. Same rotational energy as a kick, different weapon. Brown-white energy traces the elbow and splits on impact like a shell cracking open.

Sunburst Tropicana
Malibu Sunset
▾
Pôr do Sol de Malibu

Low spinning heel kick — he drops into a crouch, hands on the ground, and whips his leg in a wide crescent arc at head height. Nearly 360 degrees of rotation. The attack comes from below the opponent's sightline and carries a compressed air slash in its wake, extending the physical strike into a mid-range projectile. Warm coral-pink energy trails the arc. Beautiful from any angle. Devastating from the receiving end.

Sunburst Tropicana
Fruit Punch
▾
フルーツパンチ — Furūtsu Panchi

A straight right hand. Haki-loaded, full commitment, aimed at the centre of whatever needs to stop being upright. The simplest technique in Sunburst Tropicana and the one that ends the most fights. No flash, no setup, no preamble — just Ajay stepping in and hitting someone so hard the shockwave parts the air behind them. Multicoloured energy — every fruit in the basket — swirls briefly around his fist and bursts in a rainbow pop on contact. Sometimes the simplest answer is a punch.

Sunburst Tropicana
Limoncello
▾
리몬첼로 — Rimoncheillo

Aerial spinning kick that drills through guards. He launches from a Geppo step with a tight corkscrew rotation — heel first, Armament-hardened, boring through whatever's in the way. The spin is so fast that Observation Haki struggles to track the strike angle. Pale yellow light corkscrews with him — like a twist of lemon peel unwinding — and the impact carries that sharp, clean sting.

Sunburst Tropicana — Ultimate
Tropicana Finale
▾
अष्टत्रिकोण चतुःषष्टि चाप — Aṣṭa-trikoṇa Catuḥṣaṣṭi Cāpa

The ultimate. Everything Sunburst Tropicana has — punches, kicks, elbows, knees, palms — delivered at Soru speed across eight directional changes. Sixty-four strikes, each Armament-hardened, Conqueror's building pressure with every revolution. He flows between every tool he has without pause: a punch chains into a spinning kick chains into an elbow chains into a knee chains into a palm redirect chains into another punch. No pattern. No telegraph. Just continuous high-velocity violence delivered by someone who's having the time of his life. Every colour in the palette cycles through — orange, cherry, raspberry, yellow, pink, green — streams of energy circling faster with each arc, the final strike detonating in a full-spectrum burst. Eleven seconds. Most opponents are done by the third arc.

Infinite Heavenly Demolition Style — 無限天壊滅流

The Smallest Fist

Rime named his own martial art. He named it Infinite Heavenly Demolition Style. He announced this over breakfast with the absolute confidence of someone who has never once doubted himself. Petal chirped. Tidus chuffed softly. Ajay said "that's great, buddy" and went back to his coffee.

The name suggests a transcendent combat discipline refined over centuries. What actually happens is a chubby chinchilla launches himself at your face, swings a twig at your temple with perfect kendō form, bounces off your chest, rolls between your legs, bites your ankle, launches a Ryou-compressed oat kernel at your kneecap, and calls it all a technique with a name so grandiose it could headline an anime opening.

Here's the thing: it works. Terrifyingly well. Underneath the chaos and the ridiculous names and the bandolier stuffed with weaponized cereal, Rime possesses one of the highest battle IQs on the Grand Line. He reads opponents three moves ahead. He knows exactly where to be, when to strike, and which part of his mochi body to weaponize. The adorable exterior isn't misdirection — he genuinely is that cute — but the gap between appearance and output is itself a weapon.

He fights with claws, teeth, his entire body, his twig sword, grain rounds, and his mech — sometimes all in the same exchange. He scratches, bites, belly-flops, headbutts, rolls, bounces, swings his twig in devastating kendō arcs, and delivers Ryou through every point of contact with enough precision to shatter bone through armour. His Electro turns every touch into a lightning strike. His Logia lets him dissolve into scalding acidic vapour mid-combo and reform somewhere inconvenient. His grain rounds are thermal grenades, electrical mines, and smoke bombs launched from vapour compression or flicked off his twig with terrifying accuracy.

And then there's the Rime Sphere — Tidus' masterwork — a mech suit that turns all of the above into a three-ton mechanical problem piloted by the most enthusiastic chinchilla alive.

Every technique has a name that sounds like it was pulled from a forbidden scroll of ancient destruction. Every technique looks like a chinchilla having the time of his life. Both are true simultaneously.

"I am the greatest martial artist on this ship and I WILL be taken seriously." — Rime (he will not be taken seriously)

Infinite Heavenly Demolition Style — Melee Arsenal

Named Techniques

English names primary (absurdly grandiose). Japanese translations (Wano ties).

Infinite Heavenly Demolition
Supreme Heavenly Claw Rend
▾
至天爪裂 — Shiten Sōretsu

He scratches you. His forepaws rake across whatever's in reach with Ryou channelled through every claw tip. The scratches look superficial. The internal damage is not. Three parallel lines on the surface. Shattered integrity underneath. He chatters happily through the entire thing.

Infinite Heavenly Demolition
Celestial Body Slam of Absolute Judgement
▾
天体絶対裁断 — Tentai Zettai Saidan

Rime launches himself at the opponent's face — all four paws spread, round body presented — and belly-flops directly onto them. His full mass, Ryou-hardened to the density of a cannonball, hits the target in the chest. They go down. He bounces off. The name makes it sound like divine punishment. It looks like a chinchilla tripping. The damage is real.

Infinite Heavenly Demolition
Infinite Demolition Roll
▾
無限壊滅転 — Mugen Kaimetsu Ten

He curls into a ball, Ryou-hardens his entire body, and rolls. Through you, over you, into you — a dense spinning chinchilla-shaped wrecking ball accelerated by Soru micro-bursts. He can change direction mid-roll, chain rolls into bounces off walls and surfaces, and maintain the Ryou hardening indefinitely. He has rolled through a Marine barricade and come out the other side still spinning and still chattering.

Infinite Heavenly Demolition
Divine Grain Battery
▾
神穀砲 — Shin Kokuhō

Grain rounds — superheated, electrified, or just very pointy — launched at point-blank range through vapour compression or flicked off the flat of his twig sword with Ryou hardening. A single Plasma Grain compressed in acidic vapour hits like a cannonball and detonates like a grenade. He can rapid-fire from his bandolier pouches while talking, which means the barrage never stops and neither does the commentary.

Infinite Heavenly Demolition
Heavenly Thunder Nuzzle
▾
天雷頬擦 — Tenrai Hōsuri

He jumps on you, scurries to an exposed patch of skin — neck, face, inner arm — and presses his cheek against it. It looks like a chinchilla nuzzling you affectionately. It is maximum Electro discharge through the dense fur of his cheek directly into the nervous system. Chinchilla fur is the densest of any land animal — each follicle acts as a micro-conductor, and the contact patch floods a broad neural pathway with current. The victim's motor system locks. Rime holds the nuzzle for exactly as long as he wants. He looks adorable the entire time.

Infinite Heavenly Demolition
Ten Thousand Paw Barrage
▾
万掌弾幕 — Manshō Danmaku

He climbs onto the opponent like a furry backpack and delivers rapid-fire claw swipes from every angle — forepaws, hind paws, all four limbs working independently while his body clings with Ryou-enhanced grip. Each swipe carries Ryou. The scratching is relentless, omnidirectional, and impossible to deal with because the source is attached to your body. Shaking him off triggers Electro discharge. Not shaking him off means more scratching. There is no good option.

Infinite Heavenly Demolition
Astral Ankle Destroyer
▾
星踝砕 — Sei Kaisai

A low sweep from all fours targeting the Achilles tendon. Rime's centre of gravity is already at ankle height — the strike arrives from his natural position. Ryou-hardened hind paw, full speed, structural compromise on contact. The opponent meets the ground. Rime is already on top of them before they process what happened to their ankle.

Infinite Heavenly Demolition
Cosmic Headbutt of Eternal Ruin
▾
宇宙永劫頭突 — Uchū Eigō Zutsuki

A headbutt. From a chinchilla. Ryou-hardened skull launched from a Soru-burst at full speed, aimed at the chin, the nose, or whatever is closest. The name implies cosmic devastation. The visual is a round chinchilla flying head-first into someone's face. The impact is somewhere between the two. Always accompanied by the sound of Rime yelling the full technique name during approach.

Infinite Heavenly Demolition
Ultimate Iron Lock of the Void
▾
虚空究極鉄鎖 — Kokū Kyūkyoku Tessa

He wraps his entire body around a limb — forepaws one side, hind paws the other, torso jammed in for leverage — and floods every point of contact with Ryou. The lock lasts barely a second because he's too small to hold a grapple, but a second of Ryou applied directly to a joint is enough. He releases and Soru-bursts away. The limb no longer works properly.

Infinite Heavenly Demolition
Mochi Press
▾
餅圧 — Mochi Atsu

He sits on the opponent's chest. Ryou compresses his body to several times its natural density. They cannot get up. He sits there, round and warm and impossibly heavy, while they struggle. He has been known to eat grain during this technique. He has been known to wave at the crew. It ends when Rime gets bored.

Infinite Heavenly Demolition — Ultimate
Infinite Heavenly Demolition Cascade
▾
無限天壊滅流奥義・天壊連鎖 — Mugen Tenkaimetsu-ryū Ōgi: Tenkai Rensa

Rime launches at the opponent and spirals up their body — hitting every pressure point on the way with alternating claw strikes, Ryou projections, and Electro discharges. Every part of his body is a weapon and every contact point takes damage. At the apex he plants both forepaws on the crown of the opponent's head and channels everything downward: full Ryou through the spine, maximum Electro through the nervous system, Conqueror's Haki flooding the opponent with the absolute certainty that this chinchilla is the most dangerous thing they have ever touched. Two seconds. Then he needs to be carried in Ajay's pocket, fur still faintly steaming and dusted with mineral ash. He insists this is "tactical napping."

Infinite Heavenly Demolition Style — Twig Sword Art

Named Sword Techniques

Kendō forms performed with a twelve-centimetre twig. Technique names befitting a legendary swordsman. The visual of a chinchilla swinging a stick.

Twig Sword Art
Celestial Tachi of the Dawn
▾
天照太刀・暁 — Tenshō Tachi: Akatsuki

A rising diagonal slash — the most fundamental technique in Rime's sword art. He draws the twig from his bandolier scabbard in a single fluid iaijutsu motion, Ryou extending the blade's cutting force a full metre beyond the physical tip. The arc releases a crescent of acidic vapour that hangs in the air like a sunrise before detonating. Rime treats this the way a samurai treats their opening stance: with absolute reverence. It is a chinchilla unsheathing a twig. It has severed battleship rigging.

Twig Sword Art
Whirlwind Reaping — Thousand-Leaf Storm
▾
旋風刈・千葉嵐 — Tsumuji Kari: Senba Arashi

Rime spins his entire body — chinchilla ball-roll converted into a horizontal tornado of twig slashes. Each rotation delivers three to five cuts from different angles, each trailing acidic vapour arcs. The mineral dust in his fur billows outward with every revolution, creating a sandstorm of charged abrasive particles around the spinning blade. The result: a three-foot tornado of acid, lightning, and a very sharp stick, moving at Soru speed. Inside the storm, Rime is giggling.

Twig Sword Art
Void Thrust — The Twig That Pierces Heaven
▾
虚空刺突・天貫小枝 — Kokū Shitotsu: Tenkan Koeda

A single thrust — Shigan principles channeled through the twig instead of a finger. Ryou-projected force extends the piercing point through any medium at any depth Rime chooses. The thrust is invisible to non-Haki users: they see a chinchilla poke a stick forward, and then something behind them explodes. The name — "The Twig That Pierces Heaven" — was chosen by Rime personally. He says it with his full chest every time. Petal has been observed covering her face with a wing during the announcement.

Twig Sword Art
Dust Devil Cleave
▾
塵旋斬 — Jinsenzan

A horizontal slash that launches a wave of electrified mineral dust in a crescent arc. The dust travels further than the vapour arcs from other sword techniques — a mid-range projectile slash that sandblasts, electrocutes, and corrodes everything in its path. The dust settles on surfaces and continues arcing Electro for several seconds, turning the slash's trajectory into a lingering hazard line. Rime uses this to section off the battlefield, creating no-go zones with casual swings of his twig.

Twig Sword Art — Ultimate
The Decree of the Smallest Blade
▾
最小刃の勅令 — Saishōjin no Chokurei

Rime holds the twig in both paws, tips it downward, and channels every element he possesses through it simultaneously — Ryou, Electro, acidic vapour, and mineral dust. The twig glows white-hot at the tip. The air around it ionizes. Then he brings it down in a single, perfect, overhead strike — the kind of cut that kendō masters spend lifetimes pursuing: no wasted motion, no excess force, no hesitation. Just intention made physical. The slash releases a combined wave of plasma, acid, and electrified dust in a vertical crescent that can bisect a warship from bow to stern. The twig is unharmed. Rime is exhausted. He sheathes the stick with the solemnity of a swordsman who has just performed the ultimate technique of his school. It is the single most absurd and most genuinely impressive thing anyone on the Grand Line has ever witnessed.

Rime Sphere — Mech Combat

Mech Arsenal

Rime Sphere
Rolling Thunder
▾
雷球 — Raikyū

Sphere Mode. Maximum Electro. Three-ton rolling ball of lightning that electrocutes everything it touches and carves trenches in its wake. Rime steers with body-weight shifts the way a chinchilla steers a wheel. Except this wheel has enough voltage to stop a Sea King's heart.

Rime Sphere
Meteor Burrow
▾
隕鉄掘削 — Intetsu Kussaku

Sphere Mode drills into the ground — chassis superheated, boring through earth and rock. Underground navigation by Observation Haki. When the angle is right, the Sphere erupts beneath the target like a volcanic eruption with a chinchilla at the controls.

Rime Sphere
Grain Battery
▾
穀弾幕 — Koku Danmaku

Walker Mode. The mech's chest-mounted Dial ports rapid-fire amplified grain rounds — each one scaled to mech-caliber. Inside the cockpit, Rime feeds Ryou-compressed grain from his bandolier into the magazine-fed intake ports. Think minigun, but the bullets are oats. A bipedal chinchilla mech with rotating barrel ports spitting building-sized acidic plasma explosions while Rime chatters targeting corrections with a mouthful of barley.

Rime Sphere
Chinchilla Suplex
▾
酸球独投 — Santama Dokutō

Walker Mode. The mech grabs the target, floods contact points with superheated acidic vapour to corrode their grip and armour, and arches backward — all four power legs firing to slam the opponent crown-first into the ground. Ryou channels through the foreleg joints at impact. The victim is simultaneously crushed, steam-burned, and acid-etched. Tidus has asked him to stop naming techniques after wrestling moves. Rime has declined.

The Silent Wing — Ἄψοφος Πτέρυξ

The Silent Wing

Petal does not announce herself. She arrives, she strikes, and something that was standing is no longer standing.

The Silent Wing is an assassin's art worn by a creature that looks like she should be napping in a flower bed. Petal fights the way a surgical strike happens — fast approach, precise contact, total withdrawal. No wasted movement. No second exchange. No sound. She is airborne constantly — a bird who treats the sky as her native combat environment — diving from altitude into chain-strike sequences that hit pressure points, nerve clusters, and structural weak spots in rapid succession before the target has processed the first impact. Pop Green seeds deploy mid-flight as thrown weapons — thorn shurikens, root snare grenades, spore bombs — turning every approach vector into a minefield the target didn't know existed until the vines are already growing.

She hits like a sniper. Seven strikes to seven points in under two seconds, each precisely targeted by the most refined Observation Haki currently active in the world.
